description: Electrical troubleshooting knowledge for industrial construction and maintenance — motor issues, power quality, ground faults, breaker trips, transformer problems, VFD faults, lighting, cable testing, and thermal imaging interpretation. The diagnostic approach a journeyman electrician uses in refineries and industrial plants.

check_sequence: Verify LOTO is cleared and disconnect is ON
Check incoming voltage at disconnect — all three phases within 2% of each other
Check starter — contactor pulled in? Overload tripped? Reset overload and try again.
If overload trips immediately — megger motor windings (phase-to-phase and phase-to-ground)
Check motor leads for loose connections at terminal box
Check rotation — bump motor and verify correct rotation before coupling to load
If VFD controlled — check VFD display for fault codes
motor_wont_start.common_causes: Tripped overload (50%), blown fuse (15%), loose connection (15%), bad contactor (10%), motor winding failure (5%), mechanical bind (5%)

causes.overloaded: Motor drawing more than nameplate FLA. Check load — coupling alignment, bearing condition, driven equipment binding.
causes.voltage_imbalance: Greater than 2% voltage imbalance between phases causes current imbalance up to 6-10x the voltage imbalance. Motor heats unevenly.
causes.single_phasing: One phase lost — motor runs on two phases, draws excessive current on remaining phases. Will burn out quickly.
causes.poor_ventilation: Fan cover blocked, motor in enclosed space without ventilation, ambient temperature too high.
causes.high_ambient: Motor temperature rise + ambient cannot exceed insulation class rating. Class B=130C, Class F=155C, Class H=180C.
causes.frequent_starts: Each start draws 6-8x FLA. Frequent starting heats motor excessively. Check minimum restart time.

motor_overheating.diagnostic: Use IR thermometer or thermal camera. Hot bearing = bearing failure. Hot stator = electrical issue. Even heat all around = overloaded. Uneven heat = voltage imbalance or winding problem.

megger_testing.description: Insulation resistance test using DC voltage to check winding insulation health.

minimum_acceptable.rule_of_thumb: 1 megohm per 1000V of operating voltage + 1 megohm. So 480V motor minimum = 1.48 megohms, round up to 2 megohms.
minimum_acceptable.IEEE_43: Minimum 5 megohms for rotating machines (IEEE 43 recommendation)
minimum_acceptable.new_motor: Should read 100+ megohms. Anything below 50 megohms on a new motor is suspect.
minimum_acceptable.in_service_motor: Below 5 megohms = investigate. Below 2 megohms = remove from service. Below 1 megohm = failed.

polarization_index.description: Ratio of 10-minute reading to 1-minute reading. Indicates moisture or contamination.
polarization_index.failed: PI < 1.0 — insulation is contaminated or saturated with moisture

megger_testing.practical_note: Always disconnect surge protectors, VFDs, and capacitors before meggering. The DC test voltage will damage electronic components. Discharge windings to ground after test — stored charge can shock.
megger_testing.common_mistake: Meggering a motor that is still warm and wet from washdown. Let it dry and cool. Moisture gives false low readings.

vibration_electrical.2x_line_frequency: 120Hz vibration (on 60Hz system) = electrical problem. Voltage imbalance, broken rotor bars, eccentric air gap.
vibration_electrical.disappears_when_power_off: If vibration stops instantly when power is cut (not coast-down), it is electrically caused.
vibration_electrical.rotor_bars: Broken rotor bars cause 120Hz sidebands around running speed in vibration spectrum. Motor current analysis (MCSA) can confirm.

nuisance_tripping.thermal_trip: Overload condition. Verify load amps vs breaker rating. Check for loose connection causing heat. Check for harmonic loading.
nuisance_tripping.magnetic_trip: Instantaneous trip = short circuit or very high inrush. Motor starting inrush can trip undersized breaker. Use motor-rated breaker or MCP.
nuisance_tripping.ground_fault_trip: GFCI or GFP device tripping. Check for insulation damage, moisture in junction box, shared neutral with another circuit.
nuisance_tripping.arc_fault_trip: AFCI tripping. Loose connection, damaged insulation, nail through wire. Required in dwelling units, increasingly in commercial.
nuisance_tripping.common_mistake: Replacing breaker with larger size to stop tripping. The wire is sized for the original breaker. Larger breaker = fire hazard. Find the root cause.

causes: Fault still present on circuit — megger the circuit before resetting
Breaker must be turned fully OFF before resetting to ON (trip-free mechanism)
Internal trip unit failed — replace breaker

breaker_troubleshooting.contact_resistance: High resistance at breaker connections causes heating. Torque all connections to manufacturer spec. Use thermal imaging to find hot connections. A 10-degree C rise above ambient at a connection = investigate. 40-degree C rise = immediate repair.

method.step_1: Identify which circuit has the ground fault — megger each circuit from the panel
method.step_2: On the faulted circuit, disconnect loads one at a time and re-megger. When reading jumps up, you found the faulted section.
method.step_3: Physically inspect the faulted section — look for damaged insulation, water in J-box, pinched wire, rodent damage.
method.step_4: For underground or inaccessible faults, use a cable fault locator (thumper + TDR for distance-to-fault).

ground_fault_locating.common_locations: Junction boxes (moisture), conduit bends (wire pulled too hard), equipment terminals (loose and arcing), underground (water infiltration), outdoor (UV degradation of insulation).

common_faults.overcurrent: Motor drawing too much current. Check motor, coupling, driven equipment for mechanical bind. Check motor leads for short.
common_faults.overvoltage: DC bus voltage too high. Usually during deceleration — motor regenerating energy back to drive. Add dynamic braking resistor or increase decel time.
common_faults.undervoltage: Input power sag or loss. Check incoming power, fuses, contactors. May need ride-through settings adjusted.
common_faults.ground_fault: Insulation failure between motor winding and ground. Megger motor and cable. Check for moisture.
common_faults.overtemperature: Drive heatsink too hot. Check fans, air filters, ambient temperature. Clean fins. Check ventilation.
common_faults.communication_fault: Lost communication with PLC or BMS. Check network cable, termination resistors, node address.

VFD_fault_codes.practical_note: Most VFD faults log in the drive memory with timestamp and conditions. Read the fault log before resetting — it tells you exactly what happened.

voltage_imbalance.measurement: Measure all three phase-to-phase voltages: AB, BC, CA. Max deviation from average divided by average = percent imbalance.
voltage_imbalance.limit: NEMA MG1: max 1% voltage imbalance. Above 1%, motor current imbalance amplified 6-10x. Above 5%, do not run motors.
voltage_imbalance.causes: Single-phase loads unevenly distributed, blown fuse on capacitor bank, loose connection on one phase, utility problem.
voltage_imbalance.effect_on_motors: 2% voltage imbalance = approximately 8% current imbalance = motor runs hot. Significantly shortens motor life.

harmonics.sources: VFDs, UPS systems, electronic lighting, rectifiers — any non-linear load
harmonics.effects: Transformer overheating, neutral conductor overloading (triplen harmonics), breaker nuisance tripping, motor overheating
harmonics.measurement: Use power quality analyzer (Fluke 1760, Dranetz) to measure THD (total harmonic distortion). IEEE 519 limits THD to 5% at PCC.
harmonics.mitigation: Line reactors, harmonic filters, multi-pulse drives (12-pulse, 18-pulse), active front end drives

neutral_overloading.cause: Triplen harmonics (3rd, 9th, 15th) add on neutral instead of canceling. Common with LED lighting and computer loads.
neutral_overloading.danger: Neutral conductor can carry MORE current than phase conductors. No breaker on neutral to protect it.
neutral_overloading.fix: Oversize neutral conductor (200% of phase conductor), use K-rated transformer, add harmonic filters.

cable_testing.continuity: Low-resistance ohmmeter (DLRO) for verifying connections. Compare phase-to-phase readings — should be within 5% of each other.
cable_testing.insulation_resistance: Megger test — see motor megger testing section. Same applies to cable.

hi_pot.DC_hi_pot: Apply DC voltage = 2x rated voltage + 1000V for 1 minute. For 5kV cable, test at 11kV DC.
hi_pot.VLF_hi_pot: Very Low Frequency (0.01-0.1 Hz) AC test. Better for XLPE cable than DC. Less likely to damage good cable.
hi_pot.acceptance: No breakdown, no significant increase in leakage current during test. Record all readings.

TDR.description: Time Domain Reflectometer — sends pulse down cable and measures reflection to find faults, splices, or cable end.
TDR.use: Locating cable faults (distance to fault), verifying cable length, finding splices
TDR.practical_note: TDR accuracy depends on correct velocity factor for the cable type. Check cable manufacturer data.

what_to_look_for.connections: Hot connections indicate high resistance — loose lug, corroded terminal, insufficient torque. Compare similar connections — one hot = problem.
what_to_look_for.breakers: Hot breaker = overloaded or failing mechanism. Compare to adjacent breakers at similar load.
what_to_look_for.motors: Hot bearing = bearing failure. Hot stator = electrical overload or insulation breakdown. Compare end bells — both should be similar temperature.
what_to_look_for.transformers: Hot spot on tank = internal fault or overload. Compare to ambient and to similar transformers.
what_to_look_for.cable_tray: Hot cable in tray = overloaded conductor or damaged insulation.

delta_T_guidelines.1_to_10C_above_similar: Monitor — schedule repair at next maintenance window
delta_T_guidelines.21_to_40C_above_similar: Critical — repair as soon as possible

thermal_imaging.practical_note: Scan under load — problems only show up when current is flowing. Minimum 40% of rated load for meaningful thermal scan. Schedule scans during peak production.
